page: UpCloud CLOUDNATIVE-4xCPU-32GB is a memory-optimized virtual server running on the KVM hypervisor. It features 4 shared vCPUs powered by an AMD EPYC 7542 processor with an x86_64 architecture operating at 2.0 GHz. The hardware configuration includes 32.0 GB of system memory, yielding a high memory-to-core ratio of 8.0 GB per core. This instance does not include local storage or dedicated GPU hardware, and it is provisioned with zero complimentary public IPv4 addresses.

Benchmark data reveals a clear performance profile with distinct tradeoffs. Single-core and multi-core CPU performance tests via stress-ng rank in the bottom 10% and bottom 25% respectively, reflecting the shared CPU allocation. Similarly, Geekbench multi-core, software compilation, and ray tracing benchmarks fall into the weak category. However, memory-centric operations show more resilience; the Passmark memory score, database operations, and Redis SET operations, which achieve 5.78 million operations per second, all perform at average levels. Memory bandwidth for small cached blocks is average at 411.23 GB/sec, though it drops to weak levels for larger uncached blocks.

Given its shared CPU model and high memory density, this server offers a cost-efficient profile for memory-intensive workloads that do not require sustained, high-performance CPU compute. It is well-suited for database caching, Redis deployments, and text processing tasks. Conversely, it is poorly suited for CPU-bound tasks, static web serving, or medium-to-large language model inference, where benchmarks show poor throughput.
description: UpCloud CLOUDNATIVE-4xCPU-32GB is an x86_64 virtual server built on the KVM hypervisor, featuring 4 shared AMD EPYC 7542 vCPUs running at 2.0 GHz and 32.0 GB of RAM. Lacking local storage and dedicated GPUs, this instance is designed for memory-heavy workloads. Performance benchmarks indicate a clear tradeoff: while CPU-intensive tasks like stress-ng and Geekbench multi-core tests rank in the lower percentiles due to the shared CPU allocation, memory-bound operations perform reliably. The server achieves average ratings in Passmark database operations, Redis SET operations at 5.78 million operations per second, and small-block memory bandwidth. This configuration provides a cost-efficient option for memory-dense applications such as caching layers, Redis instances, and text processing, but is less suitable for high-throughput web serving or intensive CPU-bound computations.
